<!-- REVISED INTRODUCTION — trimmed from ~1087 to ~790 words for PNAS -->
<!-- Revision: merged P1+P2, trimmed P3 (cut rank-selection pre-emption), trimmed P4 (cut BO/alpha-0 detail) -->
<!-- Citation keys used in this file: -->
<!--   collisson2011subtypes, nguyen2024fourteen, lee1999learning, moffitt2015virtual, -->
<!--   peng2019novo, brunet2004metagenes, Bailey2016, bair2004semi, cook2007fisher, -->
<!--   rashid2020purity, peng2024determination, Schwarzova2023, collisson2019molecular, -->
<!--   bair2006supervised, arora2020survclust, aran2015systematic, -->
<!--   huang2020low, le2025survnmf, damrauer2014intrinsic, Hoadley2018, -->
<!--   gaujoux2010flexible -->
<!-- Citation keys removed from intro (still used elsewhere in paper): -->
<!--   frigyesi2008nmf (in Results), -->
<!--   snoek2012practical (in Methods/SI), tishby1999information (in Discussion), -->
<!--   chapelle2006semi (in Discussion) -->

Identifying transcriptional signatures associated with clinical outcomes has become central to molecular subtyping and prognostic stratification in cancer research [@collisson2011subtypes]. Bulk transcriptomic profiling remains the dominant technology for the large, clinically annotated cohorts required for such survival modeling, but each expression profile is a composite of malignant cells, cancer-associated fibroblasts, immune infiltrates, and other microenvironmental components [@nguyen2024fourteen]. As a result, these signatures may be diluted or obscured by variation in cellular composition across samples. Nonnegative matrix factorization (NMF) has been instrumental in resolving this mixture into additive, interpretable gene programs corresponding to recognizable cell types or transcriptional states [@lee1999learning; @moffitt2015virtual; @peng2019novo]. In pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC), this approach has yielded key biological insights, including the identification of molecular subtypes [@collisson2011subtypes], the basal-like and classical tumor programs defined through virtual microdissection [@moffitt2015virtual], and compartment-specific deconvolution of tumor and microenvironmental signals [@peng2019novo].

However, the standard approach discovers these programs through unsupervised factorization [@brunet2004metagenes] and only then evaluates their clinical relevance retrospectively [@Bailey2016; @collisson2011subtypes]. Because unsupervised NMF minimizes reconstruction error, its factors tend to be dominated by the highest-variance patterns in the data, which may reflect tissue composition or other outcome-neutral sources rather than prognostic biology [@bair2004semi; @cook2007fisher]. In PDAC, tissue-composition signals such as exocrine contamination reflect low tumor purity rather than cancer cell-intrinsic programs and carry little prognostic value [@rashid2020purity; @peng2024determination]. This misalignment between variance and prognosis extends beyond PDAC: tumor purity alone accounts for a large fraction of expression variation across cancer types [@aran2015systematic], yet the objective optimized during unsupervised discovery (reconstruction error) differs fundamentally from the criterion used during evaluation (survival association) [@cook2007fisher]. Identifying the prognostically relevant subset then requires extensive downstream filtering that is ad hoc, cohort-specific, and difficult to reproduce [@collisson2019molecular]. Over a decade of PDAC subtyping efforts proposed between two and five subtypes [@collisson2011subtypes; @moffitt2015virtual; @Bailey2016; @puleo2018stratification], ultimately converging on a robust basal/classical dichotomy confirmed by retrospective evaluation across independent cohorts [@rashid2020purity; @puleo2018stratification], single-cell resolution analyses [@chansengyue2020transcription; @werba2023single], and prospective clinical profiling [@aung2018compass].

Whether incorporating survival information during factorization improves generalizability, or risks overfitting to cohort-specific outcome distributions, remains an open empirical question. Sufficient dimension reduction theory establishes that response-guided subspace estimation targets directions most relevant to the outcome, whereas variance-maximizing projections can miss outcome-relevant structure entirely [@cook2007fisher; @bair2006supervised], and outcome-weighted methods have demonstrated this advantage in clustering and principal component settings [@bair2004semi; @arora2020survclust]. Whether this advantage extends to NMF-based deconvolution is less clear. Nonnegative constraints restrict the geometry of the factor space in ways that unconstrained SDR theory does not address; censored survival outcomes provide a noisier supervisory signal than the continuous responses assumed in supervised principal components; and survival gradients that reshape gene programs too aggressively could compromise the mixture-coefficient interpretation of sample loadings that makes NMF valuable for deconvolution in the first place. These tensions between supervision and interpretability have not been empirically resolved.

Here we present DeSurv, a survival-supervised deconvolution framework that integrates NMF with Cox proportional hazards modeling. The key architectural choice is where survival supervision enters the factorization. In DeSurv, factor scores are defined as $Z = W^\top X$, so the Cox partial likelihood is a function of the gene program matrix $W$ and regression coefficients $\beta$, and the survival gradient acts directly on gene programs. Sample-level loadings $H$ are updated solely through the reconstruction objective and receive no survival gradient, preserving their interpretation as mixture coefficients [@lee1999learning; @gaujoux2010flexible]. DeSurv operates as a semi-supervised method: a parameter $\alpha$ balances reconstruction fidelity ($1-\alpha$) and survival prediction ($\alpha$). Because $\alpha$ is selected via cross-validated concordance, any $\alpha$ large enough to overfit to cohort-specific survival patterns is penalized by poor out-of-sample performance, preventing the survival term from overwhelming the factorization. Because $W$ defines shared transcriptomic programs, new samples can be scored by projection ($Z_{\text{new}} = \tilde{W}^\top X_{\text{new}}$, where $\tilde{W}$ retains only the top-scoring genes per factor) without requiring their survival data, a property not shared by methods that route supervision through $H$ [@huang2020low; @le2025survnmf]. Hyperparameters, including factorization rank $k$ and $\alpha$, are jointly selected via cross-validated concordance (Methods).

We evaluate DeSurv in two settings. In simulations with known latent structure and survival associations, DeSurv recovers the true factorization rank and prognostic programs more reliably than standard NMF. Critically, its advantage scales with the degree of divergence between variance and prognosis and vanishes under null conditions where no survival signal exists, confirming that the method adapts to the structure of the data rather than imposing spurious survival associations. In PDAC, survival supervision reorganizes the learned factor structure: DeSurv suppresses variance-dominant but prognostically neutral exocrine-compositional signals and concentrates survival association into a smaller set of biologically interpretable factors aligned with known tumor and microenvironmental programs. These survival-aligned factors generalize across independent PDAC cohorts with consistent hazard ratios and clearer survival separation than their unsupervised counterparts---without retraining or cohort-specific tuning. These results suggest that the prognostically relevant structure in heterogeneous tumors can be recovered more reliably by aligning the discovery objective with the evaluation criterion than by post hoc filtering of unsupervised factors, potentially reducing the extensive retrospective curation currently required to identify clinically actionable transcriptional programs.
